question: IBM reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $15.9 billion and adjusted earnings above expectations, but investors reacted cautiously after the company kept its full-year outlook unchanged.
AT&T reported strong first-quarter 2026 results as Advanced Connectivity revenue grew 4.7% and wireless service revenue increased 3.6% year over year.
T-Mobile's first-quarter 2026 revenue beat expectations and the company raised its postpaid account guidance, although net income declined because of merger-related costs.
General Motors reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $43.6 billion, net income of $2.6 billion, and raised full-year adjusted EBIT guidance by $500 million.
Cognizant forecast second-quarter revenue below analyst estimates as clients remained cautious on discretionary IT spending.
Deere raised its fiscal 2026 net income outlook to $4.5 billion to $5.0 billion, but management still described the equipment market as near the bottom of a weak cycle.
PepsiCo's first-quarter 2026 net revenue increased 8.5%, organic revenue grew 2.6%, and core EPS increased as the company reaffirmed its annual guidance.
Chipotle reported first-quarter 2026 revenue growth and a surprise comparable-sales increase, but operating margin and adjusted EPS declined from the prior year.
eBay reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $3.09 billion and gross merchandise volume of $22.2 billion, both ahead of expectations.
Spotify's shares fell after the company projected second-quarter operating income below expectations because of higher AI and marketing spending.
$IBM beat Q1 revenue and EPS, but keeping the 2026 outlook unchanged makes the stock reaction feel understandable.
$T wireless and fiber growth still look solid after another quarter of stronger service revenue.
$TMUS revenue beat and guidance moved higher, but merger costs hitting net income keep the quarter from looking totally clean.
$GM raising adjusted EBIT guidance after a stronger-than-expected Q1 makes the auto story look better than feared.
$CTSH guiding Q2 revenue below the Street because clients are still cautious is not a great sign for IT services demand.
$DE raising the full-year profit outlook is nice, but calling 2026 the bottom of the cycle keeps me cautious.
$PEP showing faster revenue growth and reaffirming guidance makes the consumer-staples setup look stable.
$CMG finally beat the comp-sales fear, but lower margins and EPS mean the turnaround is still not perfect.
$EBAY revenue and GMV both beat expectations, so the marketplace comeback looks stronger than people expected.
$SPOT hitting record operating income is cool, but the next-quarter profit guide missing expectations kills the vibe.
Revenue and adjusted earnings exceeded expectations in the quarter, although our full-year outlook remains unchanged.
Advanced Connectivity revenue increased 4.7% year over year, supported by growth in wireless service revenue and fiber-related demand.
Service revenue increased double digits and account-growth guidance improved, while merger-related expenses reduced reported net income.
First-quarter EBIT-adjusted surpassed expectations, and we are raising our full-year adjusted EBIT guidance by $500 million.
We expect second-quarter revenue to come in below market expectations as clients remain cautious with discretionary technology spending.
We raised our fiscal 2026 net income forecast, while continuing to manage through weak conditions in the large agriculture cycle.
Net revenue and organic revenue accelerated in the quarter, with every business segment delivering organic growth.
Comparable restaurant sales improved modestly, but higher input and labor costs reduced operating margin and adjusted earnings per share.
Marketplace revenue and gross merchandise volume both exceeded expectations, reflecting stronger engagement across focus categories.
Operating income reached a record level in the first quarter, but planned AI and marketing investments are expected to pressure next-quarter profit.
RELEX reported using 100% renewable electricity in its offices and co-location data centers while expanding supplier ESG assessments.
Schneider Electric surpassed its 2025 sustainability target by helping customers save and avoid 862 million tons of carbon dioxide emissions.
Siemens again received a CDP Climate A List rating in 2025, signaling strong corporate climate disclosure and environmental performance.
Siemens Healthineers reported a 49% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions from its 2019 baseline.
HP reported that product-use emissions were 46% lower than 2019 levels, reflecting progress on product energy efficiency.
DHL Group reduced logistics-related greenhouse gas emissions by 4.3% in 2025 while increasing investment in sustainable fuels and technologies.
UPS powered 27.4% of its global facilities with renewable electricity in 2025 and surpassed its renewable-energy target.
Maersk said its total emissions increased by 2% in 2025 despite continued work on its energy transition.
Global corporate clean-power purchase volumes fell 10% in 2025, ending an eight-year streak of annual growth.
FedEx reported a 58% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions intensity from its FY09 baseline, reflecting progress on logistics decarbonization.
Sberbank cut Russia's 2026 GDP growth forecast to 0.5% to 1.0% after a weak first quarter.
A U.K. economic institute lowered its 2026 growth forecast to 0.9% and warned that inflation could rise to 4.1% by early 2027.
Australia's annual CPI inflation rose to 4.1% in the first quarter of 2026, keeping pressure on the central bank to raise rates.
New Zealand's central bank said first-quarter core inflation remained stable within its 1% to 3% target band.
The Swiss National Bank warned that the Middle East conflict could slow growth and raise inflation in Switzerland.
Morocco's economic growth was projected to slow to 4.0% in 2026 from 4.4% expected in 2025 as foreign demand weakened.
The IMF lowered its 2026 growth forecast for emerging market and developing economies to 3.9% as higher energy and food costs darkened the outlook.
Irish consumer sentiment fell to its lowest level since late 2022 as households grew more cautious about inflation and economic prospects.
Colombia was expected to raise interest rates again as inflation stayed well above the central bank's 3% target.
South Africa's rand fell more than 1% as investors reacted to global rate uncertainty and prolonged geopolitical risk.
